verdict_reasoning: KLK2 is canonically a secreted serine protease without any membrane-anchoring domain (no TM, no GPI). However, recent studies confirm cell-surface expression in prostate cancer cells and patient-derived tumors, with documented efficacy of CAR-T, bispecific T-cell engagers, and radioligand therapeutics that engage the protein on the cell surface. This represents a dual-localization state: the protein is predominantly secreted, but a surface pool is present and targetable in the cancer context. The surface display is not explained by an intrinsic membrane anchor mechanism but rather by a context-dependent (cancer cell-state-induced or specialized secretory/recycling pool) localization.
predicted_key_uncertainty: The molecular mechanism of KLK2 surface display remains incompletely characterized—whether this reflects cell-death-induced surfacing, secretory recycling, tumor-cell-specific uptake-and-display, or another dual-localization pathway. The surface pool is experimentally documented and therapeutically actionable, but clarity on the underlying biology would refine confidence.
